turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: We spoke about how we have two verbs for to be in Spanish, estar and ser. And we practiced with estar, which is the verb that we use for states. And we said that the word state is related to estar. Actually, it's like the same word. What looks most different there between state and estar is that / eh / that you get at the beginning of estar. But you will find this actually with words that start st-, sp-, s- and consonant. You can't begin a word like this in Spanish, where you can in English. So, actually, you will find many words between English and Spanish that get like an extra e at the beginning in Spanish. For example, the word Spanish in Spanish is Español. Or Spain, which is España. Student - estudiante. School - escuela. There you have the o splitting of school to ue. Escuela. Special - especial. Spatial:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Espacial. Strict: how do you think you might say strict, if it's the same word in English and Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Estricto. Good. Spectacular. It's the same word as well.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Espectacular. So this is the difference that we see between state and estar. So we said that estar we use with what we will consider states of being. Now with what we consider characteristics of being, we will use ser. And we said that ser, all by itself, means being. Un ser is a being. Un ser humano is a human being. Here, humano is acting like an adjective, so it's coming afterwards. But it could also be a noun. We can say the human. How would that be? The human:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: El humano. And there it's a noun. But el ser humano, there it's an adjective. So I am, from ser, is soy. Soy.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So like estoy from estar, it ends o-y. There are just four verbs in the whole spanish language that use oy instead of o, for I. Voy, doy from dar, to give, doy, I am giving, and then estoy and soy. So we said that student is estudiante. So if you wanted to say I am a student:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Soy estudiante. It is or she is, he is, you are, to usted. We learned it already, but we didn't learn that it was from ser. How would you say It is possible?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es posible. Good. So we have es. Es is from ser. Now, ser is more irregular than estar. Doesn't look like es is from ser, not too much. And then we had they are, or you guys are. What was that?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Son. Good. So, we've got three of the versions now. Soy, son, and es. We can take son and we can work out the "we" form from son, working backwards. Now we said that working backwards means taking off the consonant that you know associates to they, then bumping on the ending that associates to we, for example. So, to go from son, they are, to we are. How do you think it will sound?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Somos. Good. Which leaves one left, which is the you (informal). Which is eres. Eres. E-r-e-s.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Eres. So let's make a recap. To be:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: I am:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Soy. And if you want to use the word for I:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yo soy. He is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And if you want to use the word for he:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And she is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: They are:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Son, and what if you wanted to use the word for they? A nice way to remember they as well, is you can make she plural and you will get the feminine they.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So they are:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Ellos son, ellas son. Good. You guys are. What is you (formal)?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, and you guys is the plural of that:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ustedes. So it's the plural of usted, but it's not necessarily formal. Ustedes. Only in some parts of Spain, which we will talk about later. But everywhere else in the Spanish speaking world, this is just how you refer to you guys. It doesn't sound formal any more when it becomes plural. So how would you say you guys are?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ustedes son. Good. Then we have we are:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Somos or nosotros somos, or if we were an all female group?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Nosotras somos. Good. And then we have the you (informal), which is eres, which is probably the weirdest form out of these ones. We can relate it to es maybe, they are similar, es, eres. So how would you say you are, using the word for you?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tú eres. Good. What was the word for how?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Cómo. Now if I want to say How is he?, How is he doing? I will use estar. So how would I say, using estar, how is he, how is he doing?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Cómo está? Good. Now if I want to ask you, what is he like? Maybe you tell me about your new boss or a new friend, and I ask you, what is he like, or what is she like? Am I referring to their state or to their characteristic?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So how would I ask How is he, what is he like? How is she, what is she like?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Cómo es? Good.
